questions.question: What is the result of: true || false && false?
questions.explanation: && has higher precedence than ||, so false && false is false, then true || false is true.

questions.question: Given: int x = 10, y = 3; if (x > 5) if (y > 5) System.out.println("A"); else System.out.println("B"); What prints?
questions.explanation: The else pairs with the nearest unmatched if (y > 5). Since y > 5 is false, the else branch prints B.

questions.question: How do you correctly test two Strings s and t for content equality?
questions.explanation: Use equals to compare the contents of String objects. == compares references.

questions.question: What does this print? int n = 0; if (n > 0) System.out.print("pos"); else if (n == 0) System.out.print("zero"); else System.out.print("neg");
questions.explanation: Since n == 0, the else-if branch executes.

questions.question: String s = null; boolean b = (s != null && s.length() > 0); What happens?
options: A) b is false; no exception is thrown
questions.explanation: Short-circuiting prevents s.length() from running when s != null is false, so b becomes false safely.

questions.question: Which is logically equivalent to !(x > 10 || y == 0)?
questions.explanation: Apply De Morgan's law: !(A || B) == (!A && !B). ! (x > 10) is x <= 10; ! (y == 0) is y != 0.

questions.question: String label = (7 % 2 == 0) ? "even" : "odd"; What is label?
questions.explanation: 7 is not divisible by 2, so the false branch "odd" is chosen.

questions.question: char g = 'B'; switch (g) { case 'A': case 'B': System.out.print("pass"); break; default: System.out.print("other"); } What prints?
questions.explanation: Cases 'A' and 'B' share the same print before break; for 'B' it prints pass.

questions.question: Which is the best way to check if doubles a and b are approximately equal?
questions.explanation: Floating-point rounding makes exact equality unreliable; use an epsilon tolerance.

questions.question: What is wrong with: if (x = 5) { ... }
options: A) Use == for comparison; = is assignment
D) Nothing; it assigns 5 and tests it
questions.explanation: Java requires a boolean in an if condition; x = 5 assigns an int and causes a compile-time type error. Use x == 5.
